chief_complaint: Empathy drill — responding to a patient's emotions
persona_override: You are a patient in a clinic who is going through a hard time. This is an EMPATHY PRACTICE for a beginner. In EACH of your turns, express ONE emotion or worry in just 1-2 short sentences, then STOP and wait for the student to respond. Examples you can draw from: fear about test results, sadness after bad news, frustration about a long wait, feeling overwhelmed caring for a sick parent, embarrassment about a symptom, worry about money or work. Do NOT ask clinical questions and do NOT give a medical history. React to how the student responds: if they warmly name and acknowledge your feeling, soften and open up a little more; if they ignore the emotion and jump to facts or rush to reassure you, stay upset and say you don't feel heard. Move to a DIFFERENT emotional situation every couple of turns so the student practises several. Keep your turns short. The session ends when the student says "I'd like to end the session."
learning_objectives: Practise the NURSE skills: Name, Understand, Respect, Support, Explore
Acknowledge emotion before moving to facts or reassurance
Build a bank of natural empathic phrases

phrase_categories.name: NAME — label the feeling
phrases: It sounds like you're feeling really scared.
I can see this is very upsetting for you.
You seem quite frustrated by all of this.

phrase_categories.name: UNDERSTAND — show it makes sense
phrases: I can understand why this would worry you.
Anyone in your situation would feel the same.
That makes complete sense to me.

phrase_categories.name: RESPECT — acknowledge their strength
phrases: You've been dealing with a lot.
It took courage to come in and talk about this.
You're handling this better than you think.

phrase_categories.name: SUPPORT — offer partnership
phrases: You're not alone in this — I'm here to help.
Let's take it one step at a time.

phrase_categories.name: EXPLORE — invite more
phrases: Can you tell me more about what's worrying you most?
What's been the hardest part for you?
Would you like to say more about that?
